narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 27, well under the dataset average of 36.70. The admissions model evaluated your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, predicting a 63.9% chance that you would fail, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers, far below the average of around the middle of the pack. That low standing in your later undergraduate years pushed the model's assessment heavily toward expecting bar exam struggles, as it signals challenges in sustaining strong academic performance. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also in the lower ranks compared to the typical 5.71 average, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor, suggesting early patterns that carried forward. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA provided the strongest counterbalance, nudging the prediction toward success because, despite being below average, it showed some resilience in your overall college record. These three elements—your Year 3 decile most influential, followed by Year 1 decile, then GPA—together tipped the scales toward failure in the model's eyes, outweighing other details like your family income or full-time status. Compared to typical students in the dataset, who often rank higher in school deciles and score better on entrance exams, your academics aligned more closely with those who faced bar exam hurdles. While factors like your gender and race didn't sway the decision directly, they fit within broader patterns the model considered neutrally. To move forward, focus on bolstering your academic credentials—perhaps by retaking the LSAT to aim above 36 or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to improve your decile rankings. Building a stronger narrative around your GPA through targeted coursework or explanations in a reapplications could also help. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps head-on, so view this as a clear roadmap rather than a dead end. With persistence, you can reshape how models like this one see your potential.
